coredata.dc:title: A water-stable MOF-AgClO4-abtz as fluorescent sensor for detection of folic acid based on inner filter effect
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Bin
Li, Xinshu
Wang, Lu
An, Jundan
Wang, Tiantian
Zhang, Fei
Ding, Bin
Li, Yan
coredata.dc:description: Folic acid (FA, Vitamin B9), as a water-soluble chemical compound of the vitamin B groups, plays an important role to human health. Thus, it is of great significance to develop sensitive assays for detection of FA. Herein, we prepared a water-stable metal-organic frameworks {[Ag2(μ 2-abtz)(μ 3-abtz)(ClO4)]⋅(ClO4)} n (MOF-AgClO4-abtz; abtz = 1-(4-aminobenzyl)-1, 2, 4,-triazole) material, which exhibited a high fluorescent quantum yield of 78.97%, good water dispersibility and excellent chemical stability. Then MOF-AgClO4-abtz was developed as a “turn-off” fluorescent sensor for detection of FA via inner filter effect (IFE). The IFE mechanism was mainly originated from the spectrum overlap between fluorescence emission of MOF-AgClO4-abtz at 350 nm and UV–vis absorption band of FA ranging from 340 to 360 nm, resulting in fluorescence quenching. Furthermore, the proposed method exhibited a good linear response to FA in the concentration range of 0.1–30 μM, and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 49 nM (S/N = 3). The great reproducibility of the method was checked for three replicate detections of 20 μM FA to find relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.4%. This method also exhibited excellent long-term stability by detecting 30 μM FA for 7 days, the RSD value was obtained to be 2.8%. Ultimately, this method was employed for sensitive detection of FA in human serum and plasma samples with quantitative spike recoveries ranging from 95.3% to 104.6%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MOF-AgClO4-abtz
Fluorescent sensor
Inner filter effect
Folic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference for detection of FA
Pretreatment of the real samples collected
PXRD and optical properties of MOF-AgClO4-abtz
Optimization of the experimental conditions for detection of FA
Sensing mechanism for detection of FA
Analytical performance for detection of FA
Selectivity and anti-interference ability for detection of FA
Analytical applications for detection of FA in biological samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin City
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of MOF-AgClO4-abtz (A) at scale bar of 20 μm and (B) at scale bar of 4 μm. (C) FT-IR spectra of MOF-AgClO4-abtz and abtz ligand. (D) 2D coordination frameworks of MOF-AgClO4-abtz.
(A) Simulated PXRD pattern and experimental PXRD pattern of MOF-AgClO4-abtz, respectively. (B) Experimental PXRD patterns of MOF-AgClO4-abtz under the different pH conditions.
(A) Fluorescence excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of MOF-AgClO4-abtz at 350 nm emission and 240 nm excitation, respectively. (B) The fluorescence stability of MOF-AgClO4-abtz for 5 days.

(A) Fluorescence intensity with different concentrations (5–30 mg L−1) of MOF-AgClO4-abtz, and quenching efficiency against the different concentrations (5–30 mg L−1) of MOF-AgClO4-abtz in the presence of FA (40 μM). (B) Effect of pH on fluorescence intensity of MOF-AgClO4-abtz (10 mg L−1) and quenching efficiency of MOF-AgClO4-abtz (10 mg L−1) in the presence of FA (40 μM).

(A) Effect of FA on PXRD pattern of MOF-AgClO4-abtz. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of MOF-AgClO4-abtz (a) in the absence of FA and (b) in the presence of FA, (c) UV–vis absorption spectrum of FA, and (d) the sum absorption spectrum between MOF-AgClO4-abtz and FA. (C) Fluorescence decay curve of MOF-AgClO4-abtz (a) in the absence of FA and (b) after the addition of FA. (D) Zeta potentials of MOF-AgClO4-abtz and FA.

(A) The curve relationship diagram between quenching intensity (ΔI) of MOF-AgClO4-abtz and FA concentration in the range of 0.1–80 μM. (B) The linear relationship between quenching fluorescence intensity (ΔI) and FA concentration in the concentration range of 0.1–30 μM.

Selectivity of fluorescent sensor for detection of FA toward 40 μM different biomolecules (A) and toward 40 μM different inorganic salt ions (B). Anti-interference ability of fluorescent sensor in the presence of FA (40 μM) with various co-existing biomolecules (C) and with various co-existing inorganic salt ions (D). (I2 and Ib represented the fluorescence intensity of MOF-AgClO4-abtz/FA hybrid system with and without a co-existing substance, respectively).
Schematic diagram of the water-stable MOF-AgClO4-abtz as IFE-based fluorescent sensor for detection of FA.
Comparison of the present work with other reported methods for detection of FA.
Analytical results for determination of FA in human serum and plasma.

head.ce:title: A water-stable MOF-AgClO4-abtz as fluorescent sensor for detection of folic acid based on inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Structure and Performance for Functional Molecules, MOE Key Laboratory of Inorganic-Organic Hybrid Functional Material Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Tianjin Normal University, 393 Binshui West Road, Tianjin, 300387, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Structure and Performance for Functional Molecules
MOE Key Laboratory of Inorganic-Organic Hybrid Functional Material Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Structure and Performance for Functional Molecules, MOE Key Laboratory of Inorganic-Organic Hybrid Functional Material Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Tianjin Normal University, 393 Binshui West Road, Tianjin, 300387, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Folic acid (FA, Vitamin B9), as a water-soluble chemical compound of the vitamin B groups, plays an important role to human health. Thus, it is of great significance to develop sensitive assays for detection of FA. Herein, we prepared a water-stable metal-organic frameworks {[Ag2(μ 2-abtz)(μ 3-abtz)(ClO4)]⋅(ClO4)} n (MOF-AgClO4-abtz; abtz = 1-(4-aminobenzyl)-1, 2, 4,-triazole) material, which exhibited a high fluorescent quantum yield of 78.97%, good water dispersibility and excellent chemical stability. Then MOF-AgClO4-abtz was developed as a “turn-off” fluorescent sensor for detection of FA via inner filter effect (IFE). The IFE mechanism was mainly originated from the spectrum overlap between fluorescence emission of MOF-AgClO4-abtz at 350 nm and UV–vis absorption band of FA ranging from 340 to 360 nm, resulting in fluorescence quenching. Furthermore, the proposed method exhibited a good linear response to FA in the concentration range of 0.1–30 μM, and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 49 nM (S/N = 3). The great reproducibility of the method was checked for three replicate detections of 20 μM FA to find relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.4%. This method also exhibited excellent long-term stability by detecting 30 μM FA for 7 days, the RSD value was obtained to be 2.8%. Ultimately, this method was employed for sensitive detection of FA in human serum and plasma samples with quantitative spike recoveries ranging from 95.3% to 104.6%.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) with well-defined topological structures and fascinating physicochemical properties have received considerable attention in the field of materials chemistry. Particularly, the structural and properties features of MOFs are mainly originated from the extended frameworks matrix structure constructed by metal ions or clusters (or secondary building units (SBUs)) linked together organic ligands by strong coordination bonds [1,2]. Nowadays, MOFs have emerged as potential applications in numerous domains such as gas storage/separation [3], molecular magnetism [4], proton conductivity [5], drug delivery [6], catalysis [7], bioimaging [8] and sensing [9] because of their flexible binding sites, high surface areas, large pore volumes and adjustable pore sizes [10–12]. Remarkably, the sensing applications of luminescent MOFs have recently attracted tremendous interest in the field of analytical chemistry. As a new type of organic-inorganic hybrid material, the luminescent performance of MOFs is derived from the contribution of both metal ions and organic ligands containing aromatic or conjugated moieties. Moreover, encapsulating some guest molecules within MOFs can also give rise to luminescence [13]. Currently, MOFs have been developed as various sensing platforms for detection of analyte (or target condition) utilizing changes of the luminescent signal induced by the effect of analyte (or target condition) [14]. Some sensing applications have been reported such as pH [15], temperature [16], enzyme [17], biomolecules [18], organic solvent molecules [19], anions/cations [20] and biomarkers [21]. Nevertheless, the detection applications of most MOFs-based fluorescent sensor are always significantly restricted due to the low water solubility and poor stability, especially against an aqueous solution [22]. Therefore, it is very important to develop a water-stable MOFs as fluorescent sensor for detection assay.

Folic acid (FA), also referred to as Vitamin B9 (N-[p-{[2-amino-4-hydroxy-6-pteridinyl)methyl]-amino}benzoyl]-L-glutamic acid), which acts as an essential nutrient for numerous biological functions. During periods of new cell division and growth, FA can promote the production and maintenance of new cell [23]. Moreover, it is beneficial to the synthesis of thymidine and purine (essential components of DNA and RNA), and prevent changes to DNA, thus preventing cancer [24]. FA is also the key component of the hematopoietic system, which is considered as a coenzyme that controls the proper synthesis and production of ferroheme [25]. Thereby, the abnormal levels of FA in biological fluids can affect the physiological processes and cause some relative diseases. FA deficiency can give rise to megaloblastic anemia, cancer, leukopenia, cardiovascular disease, gastrointestinal dysfunction, fetal neural tube defects and some psychiatric disorders [26–28]. Therefore, it is essential to develop a reliable detection method for FA to help diagnose and prevent the relevant physiological diseases.

Currently, various analytical methods for detection of FA have been established such as colorimetric [24,29], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [30], surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [31,32], electrochemistry [33–35], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [36,37], and capillary electrophoresis (CE) [38]. Among these reported methods, colorimetric assay has drawn much interest due to its simplicity and low consumption, but it is restricted by poor sensitivity and strong interference from analysis samples. The ELISA is a low-cost and effective method, but the shelf-life of corresponding kits is rather short. The SERS, electrochemistry, HPLC and CE methods have their own advantages, but they also have certain drawbacks such as complex operation process, expensive equipment and time-consuming. By contrast, fluorescence analysis method is rapid and simple, highly selective and sensitive, and exhibits great application potential for detection of FA. At present, a variety of sensing materials for FA have been reported such as magnetic nanoparticles [23], quantum dots [39,40], metal nanoclusters [41], and organic fluorophores [42]. However, the applications of these materials are affected by complicated preparation/modification process, narrow linear range or relatively low sensitivity. At present, to the best of our knowledge, the application of MOFs as fluorescent sensor for detection of FA has never been reported. Moreover, the fluorescent sensor based on MOFs exhibits more fascinating advantages such as high sensitivity, short response time, simplicity, and operability [43]. Therefore, it is important to develop a MOFs material with excellent properties as fluorescent sensor for sensitive detection of FA.

Herein, a water-stable MOF-AgClO4-abtz material was prepared and developed as a fluorescent sensor for highly selective and sensitive detection of FA in biological samples. The MOF-AgClO4-abtz possessed excellent fluorescence properties (fluorescent quantum yield of 78.97%), good water dispersibility and excellent chemical stability, showing great application potential for fluorescence sensing. In this sensing assay, MOF-AgClO4-abtz was employed for effective detection of FA by quantitatively quenching its fluorescence based on inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism. The IFE quenching mechanism was ascribed to the spectrum overlap between the absorption band of FA and emission spectrum of MOF-AgClO4-abtz, and ultimately caused the emission light of MOF-AgClO4-abtz to be absorbed by absorber FA. Particularly, the IFE-based fluorescent sensors had the advantages of simplicity and flexibility with high sensitivity [44]. The proposed method could be employed for sensitive detection of FA in human serum and plasma samples with satisfactory spike recoveries, exhibiting high practicability and feasibility.

All chemical agents were analytical grade and were used without further purification. AgClO4 was purchased from Tianjin Kewei Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). 1-(4-aminobenzyl)-1, 2, 4,-triazole (abtz) was purchased from Aldrich. Ultrapure water was supplied by Wahaha Group Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Folic acid (FA) was purchased from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). MgCl2·6H2O, CaCl2, ZnCl2, NaCl, KCl, Na2HPO4, NaHCO3, NaH2PO4, Na2CO3 and Na3PO4 were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China). Tris-(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris) was purchased from Beijing Dingguo Biological Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). All amino acids were purchased from Tianjin Zhujiang Medicines and Kmart Chemical Technology Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Dopamine hydrochloride (DA) was supplied by Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO). Urea was purchased from Tianjin Fengchuan Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Oxalic acid (OX), citric acid (CA), glucose, fructose and maltose were purchased from Tianjin Kewei Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China).

Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns were collected on a D/Max-2500 X-ray diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation in the range of 5-50θ. The morphologies and particle sizes of MOF-AgClO4-abtz were characterized by Nova Nano 230 Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) at an accelerating voltage of 15 kV (FEI Company, Netherland). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectroscope (Nicolet, USA) in the range of 500–4000 cm−1 using KBr pellets. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained from a UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) in the wavelength range of 200–600 nm. The absolute fluorescent quantum yield of MOF-AgClO4-abtz at the emission wavelength of 350 nm was directly determined under the excitation wavelength of 240 nm in aqueous solutions using an FS5 photoluminescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). The fluorescence decay curves were determined on an FL-3 spectrofluorimeter (Jobin Yvon Inc., US) with excitation at 240 nm. Zeta-potential measurements were performed using a Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument (red badge, Malvern, UK) with a 633 nm He–Ne laser. The ultrasonic device used was a KQ-100E instrument with a frequency of 40 kHz and a nominal power of 100 W (Kunshan, China). All fluorescence measurements were performed on a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA) equipped with a plotter unit and a 1 cm quartz cell in fluorescence mode. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded in the wavelength range of 270–480 nm upon excitation at 240 nm. The slit widths of excitation and emission were set as 5 nm, and the photomultiplier tube (PMT) voltage was set as 720 V.

The MOF-AgClO4-abtz compound was synthesized according to our recent work [45]. A mixture of AgClO4 (20.72 mg, 0.1 mmol) and abtz (17.4 mg, 0.1 mmol) was stirred for 0.5 h in H2O (5 mL) and CH3OH (5 mL). Then the resulting solution was placed in a Teflon vessel in a steel autoclave, heated at 160 °C for 12 h and then cooled to room temperature over the period of 72 h. The resulting colorless needle-shaped crystals of MOF-AgClO4-abtz were washed several times by water and diethyl ether. Yield: 53% based on abtz. Elemental analysis calcd (%) for C18H20Ag2Cl2N8O8: C 33.21, H 3.09, N 17.21; found: C 33.45, H 3.19, N 17.43. FT-IR data (cm−1): 3195 (m), 3125 (m), 1620 (m), 1506 (m), 1428 (m), 1277 (m), 1084 (bs), 844 (m), 748 (m), 679 (m), 630 (m), 540 (m).

The MOF-AgClO4-abtz reserve suspension solution of 100 mg L−1 concentration was prepared by dispersing 3 mg of MOF-AgClO4-abtz powder in 30 mL ultrapure water under ultrasonication (80 W) for 5 min. The Tris-HCl buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.0) was obtained by diluting the 0.7880 g of Tris-(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris) in 50 mL ultrapure water and the pH was adjusted to 7.0 with pH meter. A stock solution of FA (8 mM) was prepared in ultrapure water, and a series of FA standard solutions with different concentrations was obtained by multiple dilutions of FA stock solution. Thereafter, performing the determination procedure of FA, 400 μL of 100 mg L−1 MOF-AgClO4-abtz, 400 μL of 0.1 mM Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 7.0), 3.1 mL ultrapure water were added to 4 mL of system in sequence and mixed evenly. Then, 100 μL of standard solution containing FA of different concentrations or 100 μL real sample were added to the mixed system solution for reacting 5 min. Ultimately, the fluorescence emission spectra and emission intensities at 350 nm were recorded at least three independent measurements by using a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer under the excitation wavelength of 240 nm. The fluorescence quenching intensities of MOF-AgClO4-abtz/FA hybrid system at 350 nm were applied for quantitative analysis of FA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference for detection of FA
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the selectivity and anti-interference ability of the fluorescent sensor based on MOF-AgClO4-abtz toward FA, a series of different foreign substances including organic molecules, inorganic salts and amino acids were studied for verifying the interference level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pretreatment of the real samples collected
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the practicability of the fluorescent sensor based on MOF-AgClO4-abtz in biological samples, the detection and spike recovery experiments of FA in serum and plasma were performed. Fresh human serum and plasma samples were purchased from Tianjin baiaotai Technology Development Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). The serum and plasma samples were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, then filtered to remove the suspended particles of supernatant utilizing 0.22 μm microporous membrane. Afterwards the human serum and plasma filter liquor were detected utilizing this fluorescent sensor, and the spike recoveries of FA were also carried out by adding different concentrations of FA in biological samples. Ultimately, the content of FA in serum and plasma samples, the spike recoveries and relative standard deviations (RSDs) were received.

According to our recent work [45], the MOF-AgClO4-abtz compound was successfully synthesized by hydrothermal method. Then this compound was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the SEM image showed that the crystal morphology of MOF-AgClO4-abtz product was confirmed as a relatively uniform cubic crystal shape. From the Fig. 1B, it could be clearly seen that the cubic crystal size was about 4 μm. Moreover, the FT-IR spectra of MOF-AgClO4-abtz and abtz ligand were presented in Fig. 1C. The disappearance of the characteristic peaks of –NH2 groups of MOF-AgClO4-abtz at 3410 cm−1 and 3189 cm−1 indicated that the –NH2 groups were completely coordinated with Ag ions [46,47]. The characteristic peaks of MOF-AgClO4-abtz at 1613 cm−1 and 1281 cm−1 were assigned to the N–H bending band and the stretching vibration of C–N [48], which obviously became weaker compared with that of abtz ligand. These results demonstrated that the abtz ligand reacted with Ag ions. Additionally, the characteristic peak of MOF-AgClO4-abtz at 1517 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of CN [49]. Meanwhile, the detailed coordination between Ag ions and abtz ligand could be seen from the 2D coordination frameworks of MOF-AgClO4-abtz in Fig. 1D. Obviously, the free –NH2 groups on the benzene ring and the two nitrogen atoms on the triazole ring were coordinated with Ag atoms to form the Ag–N bonds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PXRD and optical properties of MOF-AgClO4-abtz
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2 A, the experimental PXRD pattern of MOF-AgClO4-abtz powder and simulated PXRD pattern deduced from single-crystal X-ray data were compared in the 2θ range from 5° to 50°. The all diffraction peak positions of experimental PXRD pattern could coincide well with that of simulated PXRD pattern, indicating that the pure phase MOF-AgClO4-abtz was successfully synthesized. Additionally, the acid-base stability of MOF-AgClO4-abtz was explored. The MOF-AgClO4-abtz powder was immersed in the different solutions with pH values ranging from 2.0 to 10.0 for 4 h, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 2B, the PXRD patterns of these powder samples showed that the all diffraction peaks of MOF-AgClO4-abtz still remained unchanged, indicating that MOF-AgClO4-abtz possessed excellent chemical stability under the acid/base conditions. Therefore, it could be developed as a promising sensing platform for detection of analytes due to great chemical stability.

In this essay, the as-prepared luminescent MOF-AgClO4-abtz material possessed excellent fluorescence properties, and the absolute fluorescent quantum yield was determined as 78.97%. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the optimal fluorescence excitation wavelength of MOF-AgClO4-abtz was 240 nm under the emission wavelength of 350 nm, and the fluorescence emission peak at 350 nm could be obtained under the ultraviolet excitation light radiation of 240 nm. Meanwhile, the fluorescence emission spectra of MOF-AgClO4-abtz were also investigated under the different excitation wavelength in the range of 240–300 nm, which demonstrated that the fluorescence emission intensity at 350 nm was strongest under the excitation wavelength of 240 nm in the Fig. S1. Moreover, the excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of abtz ligand were explored in Fig. S2. The excitation and emission spectra of abtz ligand were similar to that of MOF-AgClO4-abtz. And the UV–vis absorption peaks of MOF-AgClO4-abtz and abtz ligand were all at 240 nm as can be observed in Fig. S3. From the above results, the fluorescence properties of MOF-AgClO4-abtz well inherited the features of abtz ligand. The fluorescence emission was mainly ascribed to π-π* electron transition of abtz ligand because there existed the chromophore containing benzene ring and triazole ring. As shown in Fig. 3B, the intensity and location of fluorescence emission peak were almost unchanged for 5 days, indicating that the MOF-AgClO4-abtz had good fluorescence stability resulting from good solvent stability and water dispersibility in aqueous solutions. Based on good chemical stability, excellent fluorescence properties and good water dispersibility of MOF-AgClO4-abtz, it can be employed as a potential fluorescent sensor for detection of analytes in biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the experimental conditions for detection of FA
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain high sensitivity of fluorescent sensor for detection of FA, the parameters of experimental conditions were optimized such as the concentration of MOF-AgClO4-abtz, pH environment, and quenching reaction time. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of MOF-AgClO4-abtz apparently increased with the concentrations increasing from 5 to 30 mg L−1. On the contrary, the quenching efficiency of MOF-AgClO4-abtz in the presence of 40 μM FA obviously decreased with the increasing concentration of MOF-AgClO4-abtz. Taking both fluorescence intensity and quenching efficiency into consideration, the 10 mg L−1 was selected as the optimal concentration of MOF-AgClO4-abtz for the follow-up detection experiments. Generally, the effect of pH environment on the sensitivity of fluorescent sensor could not be ignored in the detection system. As depicted in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensity of MOF-AgClO4-abtz was almost unchanged in the pH range of 5–9. Meanwhile, the quenching efficiency of MOF-AgClO4-abtz in the presence of 40 μM FA was not affected by different pH environments. This result could be ascribed to good chemical stability of MOF-AgClO4-abtz. Considering pH environment of the biological fluids, the optimal pH value was chosen as 7.0 for sensitive detection of FA. Additionally, the stabilization time of fluorescence quenching was also optimized for ensuring the availability and accuracy of fluorescence data. It could be clearly seen from Fig. S4 that the fluorescence intensity of MOF-AgClO4-abtz decreased quickly within a short time, which may be ascribed to the rapid response of the IFE-based MOFs fluorescence sensing means. Then the fluorescence quenching intensity tended to stabilize and reached equilibrium until the reaction time came up to 5 min. Therefore, 5 min was selected as the optimal quenching reaction time for detection of FA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism for detection of FA
ce:section.ce:para: The exploration on quenching mechanism of MOFs is of great significance for strategy design of sensing system in analytical assay. For “turn-off” sensing, the fluorescence quenching mechanism is mainly derived from the formation of non-fluorescent ground state complex, photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE) [10,44]. In general, the formation of ground state complex is attributed to static quenching with changing ultraviolet absorption of sensing material, and its fluorescence lifetime is not affected by analyte [50,51]. Conversely, the change of fluorescence lifetime of sensing material induced by analyte is ascribed to dynamic quenching [10]. Furthermore, PET, as a redox process, will result from a molecule in the electronic excited state that can donate or capture electrons of fluorophore [52], and result in fluorescence quenching. Particularly, both FRET and IFE mechanisms are energy transfer processes, but there are still significant differences between them. FRET is that the energy of excited donor fluorophore is transferred to a nearby acceptor molecule via a non-radiative pathway [53]. It is mainly originated from the spectrum overlap between the absorption of the acceptor analyte and emission of the donor fluorophore, and there need to exist the appropriately relative dipoles orientation and distance between donor and acceptor [54]. Meanwhile, FRET should be attributed to dynamic quenching with changing fluorescence lifetime [55]. Compared with FRET, IFE is a non-irradiation energy conversion model by converting the absorption signals into fluorescence signals, which mainly results from the spectrum overlap between absorption of analyte and excitation and/or emission band of fluorophore [56]. Particularly, the IFE-based sensing assay does not require covalent linking or intermolecular interactions between fluorophore and absorber [57]. Importantly, the IFE mechanism does not belong to static or dynamic quenching [58]. So, it is important to infer the actual quenching mechanism of the sensor for MOFs sensing assay.

In this assay, in order to gain deeper insight into the fluorescence quenching mechanism of this detection system, a series of measurements had been carried out including PXRD analysis, UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence lifetimes and zeta potentials. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the PXRD pattern diffraction peaks of MOF-AgClO4-abtz/FA hybrid system remained unchanged and matched well with those of the simulated and as-prepared MOF-AgClO4-abtz. The results clearly showed that the FA did not affect the framework structures of MOF-AgClO4-abtz during detection process. The quenching mechanism caused by structural collapse of MOF-AgClO4-abtz was firstly excluded [20].

As can be seen from the UV–vis spectra measured in Fig. 5B, there existed a strong absorption peak at 240 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of MOF-AgClO4-abtz (curve a). The FA molecule had two absorption peaks at 280 nm and 350 nm (curve c). Additionally, the sum UV–vis absorption spectrum (curve d) between MOF-AgClO4-abtz and FA molecule was overlapped with the single UV–vis absorption spectrum (curve b) of MOF-AgClO4-abtz/FA hybrid system within experimental error. Thus it was proven that there was no formation of ground state complex and it did not involve static quenching [55], indicating that the binding of FA on the surface of MOF-AgClO4-abtz was ascribed to physical absorption [59]. As shown in Fig. S5, there existed the spectrum overlap between the fluorescence emission of MOF-AgClO4-abtz at 350 nm and the UV–vis absorption band of FA molecule in the wavelength of 340–360 nm, thus the possible fluorescence quenching mechanism was attributed to FRET or IFE mechanism [60]. In order to further confirm the actual quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime of MOF-AgClO4-abtz was measured. The fluorescence decay curves were well fitted with biexponential equation, and the average fluorescence lifetimes were calculated by the sum of the ∑b i τ i products. According to fluorescence decay components listed in Table S1, the average fluorescence lifetime of MOF-AgClO4-abtz was about 1.150 ns. With the addition of FA, the average fluorescence lifetime was changed to 1.126 ns. Meanwhile, the fluorescence decay curves and the average fluorescence lifetimes are exhibited in the Fig. 5C. The results further indicated that the fluorescence lifetime of MOF-AgClO4-abtz still remained unchanged in the presence of FA. So the fluorescence quenching mechanism was unreasonable ascribed to the FRET [61]. Additionally, for FRET, the distance between donor fluorophore and acceptor analyte should be less than 10 nm [62]. As illustrated in Fig. 5D, the results showed that the average zeta potentials of MOF-AgClO4-abtz and FA were determined as −8.8 mV and −41.6 mV, respectively. Therefore the distance between MOF-AgClO4-abtz and FA hardly shorter than 10 nm due to the repulsive electrostatic interaction of them, which did not conform the applicable conditions of FRET mechanism. On the basis of the above results, the quenching mechanism could be ascribed to IFE mechanism. In this IFE-based sensing assay, the absorption degree of the FA could adjust the fluorescence emission signal of MOF-AgClO4-abtz due to the spectrum overlap between the absorption band of FA and the emission band of MOF-AgClO4-abtz, so as to indirectly realize the conversion between the concentration of FA and the emission signal, and ultimately achieve the detection purpose of FA.

In brief, as shown in Scheme 1 , MOF-AgClO4-abtz could generate excellent fluorescence emission at 350 nm under the excitation wavelength of 240 nm. Then with the addition of FA, the fluorescence of MOF-AgClO4-abtz was gradually quenched. The quenching mechanism was ascribed to IFE due to the spectrum overlap between the UV–vis absorption band of FA ranging from 340 to 360 nm and fluorescence emission of MOF-AgClO4-abtz at 350 nm. Ultimately, the emission light of MOF-AgClO4-abtz was absorbed by absorber FA, resulting in quenching phenomenon. Moreover, this method showed a good linear relationship between the quenching intensities (I–I0) and FA concentrations varying from 0.1 to 30 μM. This proposed method exhibited great application potential for detection of FA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance for detection of FA
ce:section.ce:para:
The influence of FA concentration on fluorescence intensity of MOF-AgClO4-abtz was investigated under the optimal experimental conditions. From the Fig. S6, the fluorescence intensity of MOF-AgClO4-abtz/FA hybrid system at 350 nm gradually decreased with the increasing concentration of FA in the range of 0.1–80 μM. As can be seen in Fig. 6 A, the change of fluorescence intensity (I–I0, ΔI) of MOF-AgClO4-abtz exhibited a non-linearity response toward FA concentration varying from 0.1 to 80 μM. And the non-linearity behavior of the sensing method in the large concentration range of FA could be related to the IFE mechanism [63]. Nevertheless, the changes of fluorescence intensity (I–I0, ΔI) showed a good linear relationship against the FA concentration (C) ranging from 0.1 to 30 μM. This fitting linear equation could be expressed as
ΔI = I −
I 0
= 13.14 C + 6.27
with R2 = 0.994 in the Fig. 6B, where I and I0 were the fluorescence intensities of the MOF-AgClO4-abzt at 350 nm in the presence and absence of FA, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) for FA was calculated to be 49 nM (S/N = 3) based on 3δ/κ (δ was the standard deviation for the blank sample measurements, and κ was the slope of the fitting linear equation). And the reproducibility of fluorescent sensor was checked for three replicate detections of 20 μM FA to find relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.4%. The above analytical characteristic results are listed in Table S2. Additionally, as shown in Fig. S7, this fluorescent sensor was employed to detect 30 μM FA for 7 days to find RSD value of 2.8%, indicating that this method had excellent long-term stability. Furthermore, as shown in Table 1 , the features of this fluorescent sensor including sensing material, linear range and LOD were compared with those of other reported methods [23,24,29,31–37,39–41]. The analysis result demonstrated that our method presented a relatively wide linear range and a low LOD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference ability for detection of FA
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity and anti-interference ability are two important evaluation indicators of detection performance in biological samples. Thus the selectivity and anti-interference experiments of this fluorescent sensor for detection of FA were performed under the optimal experimental conditions. In the assay of selectivity, the fluorescence intensities (I1) of MOF-AgClO4-abtz were recorded at 350 nm in the presence of the different substances, respectively. These different substances contained organic molecules (FA, OX, CA, DA, Glucose, Fructose, Urea, Maltose), amino acids (Asp, Glu, Phe, Met, Lys, Asn, Val, His, Ile, Gln, Thr, Pro, Ser, Gly, Ala, Leu, Arg), and inorganic salt ions (PO4 3−, CO3 2−, Zn2+, Ca2+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, HCO3 −, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−) and 40 μM was chosen as the concentration of all different substances. The blank fluorescence intensities (I0) of MOF-AgClO4-abtz were also recorded at 350 nm. As illustrated in Fig. 7 A and B, no substances except FA had significant effect on the ratio value of I1/I0, indicating that only FA have the significant quenching behavior toward to the fluorescence of MOF-AgClO4-abtz.

Generally, it is considered that the various co-existing interfering substances cannot interfere for FA detection when the calculated relative error with respect to the blank is less than ±5.0% [64]. As seen from the ratio value of I2/Ib of Fig. 7C and D, the fluorescence quenching intensity of MOF-AgClO4-abtz in the presence of 40 μM FA had not been significantly interfered by the above co-existing substances, respectively. Meanwhile, as shown in Tables S3 and S4, the tolerable concentrations of the different co-existing interfering substances for 40 μM FA ranged from 200 to 20000 μM. Additionally, the quenched fluorescence changes (%) of the detection system for 40 μM FA ranged from −4.52% to 3.00% with an error tolerance of 5.0% based on the ratio value of I2/Ib, indicating that these commonly interfering substances could not cause any serious interference in the detection process of FA. All these analysis results revealed that the fluorescent sensor based on MOF-AgClO4-abtz for detection of FA have outstanding selectivity and high anti-interference ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical applications for detection of FA in biological samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further evaluate the practicability of the fluorescent sensor in biological samples, the detection assay of FA was performed in two serum samples and two plasma samples. As shown in Table 2 , this fluorescent sensor was employed for detection of FA in real samples, and we also studied through the spike recovery method the sensor efficiency for FA determination. The recovery experiments were performed by quantitatively spiking a series of different known concentration of FA (5 and 10 μM) in the serum and plasma samples. The received quantitative spike recoveries for detecting FA in the serum and plasma samples ranged from 95.3% to 104.6% with relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) ranging from 0.4% to 4.6%. From all the above, the fluorescent sensor based on MOF-AgClO4-abtz material was reliable for detection of FA in biological samples.

Nowadays the researches on sensing applications of MOFs materials for biomolecules had received great attentions. This essay reported that a water-stable MOF-AgClO4-abtz possessed good fluorescence stability, great water dispersibility and excellent fluorescence properties (fluorescent quantum yield of 78.97%), and exhibited good chemical stability under the acid/base conditions. Simultaneously, it was successfully employed to develop as an IFE-based fluorescent sensor for detection of FA in serum and plasma samples with satisfactory spike recoveries. The proposed method could avoid the complicated process of modification/functionalization toward MOFs material, and can be directly employed for FA determination with low consumption of reagents and an operation, simple, rapid and sensitive. The developed IFE-based fluorescent sensor based on MOF-AgClO4-abtz material exhibited great applicability and practicability for detection of FA in biological samples.

Bin Yang: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Xinshu Li: Software. Lu Wang: Investigation. Jundan An: Validation. Tiantian Wang: Visualization. Fei Zhang: Project administration, Visualization, Supervision. Bin Ding: Resources, Formal analysis. Yan Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Software, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the financial support from the Open Project of Key Lab Adv Energy Mat Chem (Nankai Univ), Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin (18JCYBJC89700), National Natural Science Foundation of China (20975054, 21301128).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2020.121019.
